---
title: "POSSE & Webmentions on a Static Quarto Site"
abstract: |
  The IndieWeb movement offers a compelling answer to social media fatigue: own
  your content, syndicate it outward, and let the web itself carry the
  conversation back to you. In this post I walk through how I implemented
  POSSE and Webmentions on this site — a fully static Quarto website hosted on
  GitHub Pages — with no backend, no database, and no third-party JavaScript
  framework.
date: 2026-03-15
author:
  - name: Guillaume Gilles
  - name: Claude Sonnet 4.6
    url: https://www.anthropic.com/claude/sonnet
    roles:
      - reviewing code
      - writing - review & editing
image: image.png
word-count: true
categories: [IndieWeb, Quarto, Web]
---

## The problem with social media as infrastructure

Every time you publish a thought on LinkedIn, a thread on a forum, or a reply
on a social platform, you are building on rented land. The platform controls
discovery, the platform controls the archive, and the platform can — and
does — change the rules. Accounts get suspended, APIs get restricted, companies
get acquired.

The [IndieWeb](https://indieweb.org/) movement's answer is simple and old:
**own your content.** Publish first on a domain you control, then push copies
outward. The acronym for this workflow is **POSSE**: *Publish on your Own Site,
Syndicate Elsewhere.*

But owning the canonical post is only half the equation. If someone replies to
your LinkedIn copy, that conversation is trapped on LinkedIn. **Webmentions**
close that loop: they are a W3C-standardised protocol that lets any website
notify another that it has linked to or responded to it. Combined, POSSE +
Webmentions give you a personal site that is the source of truth *and* an
active participant in distributed conversations.

## How POSSE works

The POSSE workflow is deliberately low-tech:

1. **Write and publish on your own site first.** The URL on your domain is the
   canonical URL — the one you want search engines, archives, and readers to
   remember.
2. **Syndicate copies to platforms** — LinkedIn, Mastodon, a newsletter, an
   aggregator — each copy links back to the original with a `rel="canonical"`
   or just a plain "Originally published at…" line.
3. **Collect responses.** Ideally, replies on those platforms flow back to your
   site via Webmentions (more on that below). At minimum, you know the
   conversation happened because you own the thread.

The key insight is the direction of authority: the copy points to the original,
never the other way around. Your domain is the source; social media platforms
are distribution channels.

For this site, the technical foundation of POSSE is already in place:

- **`site-url: https://ggilles.dev`** in `_quarto.yml` — Quarto embeds this
  as the canonical base URL in every page's `<head>`, so syndicated copies can
  always point home.
- **RSS feed** at `/blog.xml` — any reader or aggregator can subscribe
  directly without going through an algorithm.

## How Webmentions work

[Webmention](https://www.w3.org/TR/webmention/) (W3C Recommendation, 2017) is
a simple HTTP protocol. When site B publishes a post that links to a URL on
site A, site B's publishing software sends a POST request to site A's
webmention endpoint. Site A receives the notification, verifies that the link
is real, and can display it however it likes.

The full flow looks like this:

```
Site B publishes a post linking to https://ggilles.dev/posts/my-post/
  ↓
Site B (or a service) POSTs to https://webmention.io/ggilles.dev/webmention
  with source=https://siteB.com/their-post & target=https://ggilles.dev/posts/my-post/
  ↓
webmention.io verifies the link exists in site B's page
  ↓
webmention.io stores the mention
  ↓
ggilles.dev fetches the mention from the webmention.io API and displays it
```

Three actors are involved:

| Actor | Role |
|---|---|
| [webmention.io](https://webmention.io) | Receives and stores incoming webmentions for your domain |
| [Bridgy](https://brid.gy) | Polls social platforms (LinkedIn, Mastodon…) and converts likes, reposts, and replies into webmentions |
| Your site | Declares the endpoint; fetches and renders the stored mentions |

## Implementation on a static Quarto site

A static site cannot receive HTTP POST requests, which is why webmention.io
exists: it acts as your inbox. Your site only needs to advertise the endpoint
in its `<head>` and fetch the stored data via a read API.

### Step 1 — Declare your identity and endpoint

Create `assets/head-indieweb.html`:

```html
<!-- IndieWeb: webmention endpoint -->
<link rel="webmention" href="https://webmention.io/ggilles.dev/webmention" />
<link rel="pingback"   href="https://webmention.io/ggilles.dev/xmlrpc" />

<!-- IndieAuth: rel="me" identity links -->
<link rel="me" href="https://github.com/guillaumegilles" />
<link rel="me" href="https://www.linkedin.com/in/guillaumegilles" />
```

The `rel="webmention"` tag tells any Webmention-aware sender where to deliver
notifications. The `rel="me"` tags are used by [IndieAuth](https://indieauth.com/)
to verify that the profiles listed belong to the owner of `ggilles.dev` — a
prerequisite for signing in to webmention.io without a password.[^indieauth]

[^indieauth]: IndieAuth works by cross-referencing: your site links to your
  GitHub profile with `rel="me"`, and your GitHub profile links back to your
  site. This mutual link proves ownership.

Add this file to `_quarto.yml` so it is injected into every page:

```yaml
format:
  html:
    include-in-header: assets/head-indieweb.html
```

### Step 2 — Sign up at webmention.io

Go to [webmention.io](https://webmention.io) and sign in with your site's URL.
webmention.io will look for the `rel="me"` links in your `<head>` and verify
your identity via IndieAuth. Once confirmed, it starts accepting mentions
addressed to your domain.

This is a one-time, zero-configuration step. webmention.io is free for
personal sites.

### Step 3 — Wire up Bridgy for social backfeeding

[Bridgy](https://brid.gy) is the bridge between siloed social platforms and
the open web. Connect it to your LinkedIn, Mastodon, or other accounts and it
will:

1. Poll those platforms for likes, reposts, and replies on posts that link to
   your site.
2. Convert each interaction into a Webmention and send it to webmention.io.

No code required — it is a point-and-click setup on brid.gy. Once connected,
social interactions flow automatically into your webmention.io inbox.

### Step 4 — Display webmentions on posts

The webmention.io read API is public. A small JavaScript snippet — included
once and applied to every post — fetches mentions for the current page URL and
renders them.

Create `assets/webmentions.html`:

```html
<section id="webmentions">
  <h2>Webmentions</h2>
  <div id="wm-facepile"></div>
  <div id="wm-replies"></div>
  <p id="wm-empty" style="display:none;">No webmentions yet.</p>
</section>

<script>
(function () {
  const LIKES   = ["like-of", "bookmark-of"];
  const REPOSTS = ["repost-of"];
  const REPLIES = ["in-reply-to", "mention-of"];

  const target = encodeURIComponent(window.location.href);
  const api    = `https://webmention.io/api/mentions.jf2?target=${target}&per-page=100`;

  fetch(api)
    .then(r => r.json())
    .then(data => {
      const mentions = data.children || [];
      if (!mentions.length) {
        document.getElementById("wm-empty").style.display = "block";
        return;
      }
      const facepile  = document.getElementById("wm-facepile");
      const repliesEl = document.getElementById("wm-replies");

      mentions.forEach(m => {
        const type   = m["wm-property"];
        const author = m.author || {};
        // render likes/reposts as avatars, replies as cards
        // … (see full source below)
      });
    });
})();
</script>
```

The API returns a [JF2](https://www.w3.org/TR/jf2/) feed — a JSON format for
social interactions. Each entry has a `wm-property` field that describes the
interaction type:

| `wm-property` | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `like-of` | Someone liked the post |
| `bookmark-of` | Someone bookmarked it |
| `repost-of` | Someone reposted or shared it |
| `in-reply-to` | A direct reply |
| `mention-of` | The post was mentioned but not directly replied to |

Likes and reposts are rendered as a compact **facepile** — a row of avatar
images. Replies and mentions get a full **reply card** with the author's name,
date, and a preview of their text.

Tell Quarto to append this widget to every post by adding one line to
`posts/_metadata.yml`:

```yaml
format:
  html:
    include-after-body: ../assets/webmentions.html
```

`_metadata.yml` in a subdirectory applies to all documents in that directory
and its children — so this single line adds the widget to every post without
touching individual `.qmd` files.

## The full picture

Here is how all the pieces fit together in the final architecture:

```
You write a post → quarto render → push to GitHub Pages
                                         │
                    ┌────────────────────┘
                    │ <link rel="webmention" href="webmention.io/…" />
                    │ <link rel="me" href="github.com/guillaumegilles" />
                    │ RSS feed at /blog.xml
                    └→ ggilles.dev (canonical source)

You share the post URL on LinkedIn / Mastodon
                    └→ Bridgy polls LinkedIn
                         └→ sends Webmention to webmention.io
                              └→ stored in webmention.io inbox

Reader opens a post on ggilles.dev
                    └→ webmentions.html JS runs
                         └→ GET webmention.io/api/mentions.jf2?target=…
                              └→ renders likes, reposts, replies inline
```

No server, no database, no build-time API calls. The canonical content is
fully static; social interactions are fetched client-side on demand.

## Sending webmentions when you publish

Receiving webmentions is automatic once the endpoint is declared. Sending them
— notifying other sites that you have linked to them — requires an extra step.

The simplest approach is [webmention.app](https://webmention.app/): a free
service that polls your RSS feed for new posts and sends webmentions to every
URL you link to. Point it at `https://ggilles.dev/blog.xml` and it handles the
rest.

Alternatively, you can trigger sends manually with the [`webmention`
CLI](https://www.npmjs.com/package/webmention) or add a GitHub Actions
workflow that runs on every push to `main`.

## What does not require any of this

POSSE does not require Webmentions to be useful. Even without the feedback
loop, the discipline of publishing on your own domain first — and treating
social platforms as mirrors rather than originals — gives you:

- A permanent, searchable archive you control.
- A canonical URL to link to from anywhere.
- Independence from any single platform's algorithm or terms of service.

Webmentions are the layer that makes the distributed conversation visible.
They are a nice-to-have that upgrades the experience significantly if the
people engaging with your content are on IndieWeb-aware platforms (Mastodon,
personal blogs running WordPress with the Webmention plugin, etc.).

LinkedIn and most corporate platforms will likely never implement Webmention
natively — that is what Bridgy is for.

## Replicating this on your own Quarto site

The full implementation is four files and two external accounts:

**Files**

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `assets/head-indieweb.html` | Webmention endpoint + `rel="me"` identity |
| `assets/webmentions.html` | Client-side display widget |
| `posts/_metadata.yml` | Auto-include widget on all posts |
| `_quarto.yml` | `include-in-header`, `site-url` |

**External accounts (both free)**

| Service | What it does |
|---|---|
| [webmention.io](https://webmention.io) | Your webmention inbox |
| [brid.gy](https://brid.gy) | Social platform backfeeding |

Replace every occurrence of `ggilles.dev` with your own domain and every
social profile URL with yours. The rest is generic and reusable as-is.

## References

- [IndieWeb: POSSE](https://indieweb.org/POSSE)
- [W3C Webmention Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/TR/webmention/)
- [JF2 Post Serialization Format](https://www.w3.org/TR/jf2/)
- [webmention.io](https://webmention.io) — Aaron Parecki
- [Bridgy](https://brid.gy) — Ryan Barrett
- [Quarto: `include-in-header`](https://quarto.org/docs/output-formats/html-basics.html#includes)
- [Quarto: `_metadata.yml`](https://quarto.org/docs/projects/quarto-projects.html#directory-metadata)
